Alexandre Ghiti

1 items
Patches Submitted (1)
Last comment 2026-05-11 [PATCH 0/8] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting 8 patches
Show 8 individual patches
  • [1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats Neutral Last comment 2026-05-11 Shakeel Butt
  • [2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
  • [3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
  • [4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
  • [5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
  • [6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
  • [7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
  • [8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
Neutral LLM

This patch series aims to introduce per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting, allowing for accurate tracking of kernel memory usage on a per-node basis. The patches modify various parts of the Linux kernel to implement this new accounting mechanism, including changes to memcontrol, slab, and zswap.

0 participants
Shakeel Butt ↳ replying to Alexandre Ghiti Acked-by Neutral LLM
Requested clarification on a specific sentence in the commit message and suggested minor code formatting changes. Provided an Acked-by after the issues were addressed.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 10:20:36PM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> flush_nmi_stats() drains per-node NMI slab atomics into the per-node
> lruvec_stats, but does not propagate them to the memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> This is inconsistent with account_slab_nmi_safe() which updates both,

I think the above sentence needs clarification. Something like "For non nmi
case, account_slab_nmi_safe() calls mod_memcg_lruvec_state() which updates both
per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, so flush_nmi_stats() needs to
flush to per-node lruvec_stats as well as memcg-level vmstats but at the moment
the memcg-level vmstats flushing is missing. Fix that".

> so fix this by propagating the NMI slab stats to the memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> Fixes: 940b01fc8dc1 ("memcg: nmi safe memcg stats for specific archs")
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  mm/memcontrol.c | 6 ++++++
>  1 file changed, 6 insertions(+)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> index c3d98ab41f1f..d81a76654b2c 100644
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
> @@ -4341,16 +4341,22 @@ static void flush_nmi_stats(struct mem_cgroup *memcg, struct mem_cgroup *parent,
>  			int index = memcg_stats_index(NR_SLAB_RECLAIMABLE_B);
>  
>  			lstats->state[index] += slab;
> +			memcg->vmstats->state[index] += slab;
>  			if (plstats)
>  				plstats->state_pending[index] += slab;
> +			if (parent)
> +				parent->vmstats->state_pending[index] += slab;

Nit: please keep all three code lines additions together.

>  		}
>  		if (atomic_read(&pn->slab_unreclaimable)) {
>  			int slab = atomic_xchg(&pn->slab_unreclaimable, 0);
>  			int index = memcg_stats_index(NR_SLAB_UNRECLAIMABLE_B);
>  
>  			lstats->state[index] += slab;
> +			memcg->vmstats->state[index] += slab;
>  			if (plstats)

Same here.

>  		}
>  	}
>  }

Boris Burkov

19 items
Patches Submitted (10)
Last comment 2026-05-11 [PATCH v2] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock before cond_resched() Neutral LLM

The patch addresses a lock contention issue in the btrfs extent map shrinker, which can cause memory pressure due to kswapd getting stuck in inode slab shrinking. The fix involves replacing `cond_resched_lock()` with an unconditional unlock/lock around `cond_resched()` to decouple lock sharing from CPU sharing.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Filipe Manana Reviewed-by Positive LLM
Identified the issue as a lock contention problem and confirmed that the proposed fix resolves it. Provided a formal Reviewed-by tag.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 7:24 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
>
> find_first_inode() and find_first_inode_to_shrink() lock root->inodes,
> then loop over them, occasionally skipping some inodes. When they skip
> an inode, they attempt to share the cpu/lock with cond_resched_lock().
>
> However, that has a subtle problem associated with it.
> cond_resched_lock() only drops the lock if it needs to actually call
> schedule(). With CONFIG_PREEMPT_NONE, this means the full timeslice as
> detected at ticks. With 8+ cpus and default tunables, this is 2.8ms. So
> regardless of HZ, we will run for at least 2.8ms in this loop without
> dropping the lock, assuming it finds no suitable inodes. If HZ is
> small enough, it might be even worse as the tick granularity becomes
> bigger than the timeslice.
>
> The knock-on effect of this is that callers to
> btrfs_del_inode_from_root() like kswapd trying to shrink the inode slab
> or userspace threads calling evict() will spin on xa_lock(&root->inodes)
> for 2.8ms, so the extent map shrinker dominates the lock even though
> ostensibly it is intending to share it. This produces memory pressure as
> there is only one kswapd and it runs sequentially so it can get stuck in
> the inode slab shrinking.
>
> To fix it, simply replace cond_resched_lock() with an open coded variant
> which unconditionally does unlock/lock around cond_resched. Sharing the
> lock is decoupled from sharing the cpu, and all the users of the lock
> now share it fairly.
>
> I was able to reproduce this on test systems by producing a lot of empty
> files (to make a big root->inodes xarray), then producing memory
> pressure by reading large files larger than ram, triggering kswapd and
> the extent_map shrinker. The lock contention is visible with perf or
> lockstat. This patch also relieved a user-apparent bottleneck on a
> production system from the original report.
>
> Tested-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

Looks good, thanks.

> ---
> Changelog:
> v2:
> - apply the pattern to btrfs_find_first_inode() as well.
>
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/extent_map.c | 4 +++-
>  fs/btrfs/inode.c      | 4 +++-
>  2 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> index 514d7b59cbe5..fce9c5cc0122 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> @@ -1246,7 +1246,9 @@ static struct btrfs_inode *find_first_inode_to_shrink(struct btrfs_root *root,
>                 write_unlock(&tree->lock);
>  next:
>                 from = btrfs_ino(inode) + 1;
> -               cond_resched_lock(&root->inodes.xa_lock);
> +               xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> +               cond_resched();
> +               xa_lock(&root->inodes);
>         }
>         xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/inode.c b/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> index 0e72408c8612..201010ced09d 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> @@ -10540,7 +10540,9 @@ struct btrfs_inode *btrfs_find_first_inode(struct btrfs_root *root, u64 min_ino)
>                         break;
>
>                 from = btrfs_ino(inode) + 1;
> -               cond_resched_lock(&root->inodes.xa_lock);
> +               xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> +               cond_resched();
> +               xa_lock(&root->inodes);
>         }
>         xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
>
> --
> 2.54.0
>
>
Signals: LGTM
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-10 Last comment 2026-05-11 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock in find_first_inode_to_shrink() Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch fixes a lock contention issue in the Btrfs extent map shrinker by always dropping the root->inodes lock in the find_first_inode_to_shrink() function. The original code would sometimes spin on the lock for up to 2.8ms, causing memory pressure and performance issues. The patch replaces the cond_resched_lock() call with an unconditional unlock/lock around cond_resched(), ensuring that the lock is shared fairly among users. This fix was tested and verified by the submitter and another developer.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants

Why do that only here?

Thanks.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-12 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: squota lifetime fixes Neutral LLM

This patch series aims to strengthen the enforcement of a crucial invariant in btrfs squotas: 'a squota qgroup must outlast extents that are accounted to it'. It consists of two fixes for squota lifetime bugs and two patches that reduce the consequences of errors in lifetime management. The series also includes additional improvements to btrfs check and fstests.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Qu Wenruo Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Identified potential issues in the last two patches and suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to catch real bugs for debug builds. Considered the patch series overall and found it looks good, but minor comments were made.
Show original comment 

在 2026/5/13 05:04, Boris Burkov 写道:
> Simple quota accounting can undercount metadata tree block allocations
> in certain scenarios. When an undercounted subvolume is deleted and its
> tree blocks freed, the free deltas decrement rfer/excl past zero,
> wrapping the u64 to a value near U64_MAX.
> 
> Once wrapped, can_delete_squota_qgroup() sees non-zero rfer and refuses
> to delete the qgroup. The qgroup becomes permanently orphaned in the
> quota tree, since there is no subvolume left to generate frees that
> would bring the counter back to zero.
> 
> While we ultimately want to fix any mis-accounting at the source, it is
> also helpful and worthwhile to mitigate the damage by clamping rfer and
> excl to zero on underflow rather than allowing the u64 to wrap. This at
> least allows us to clean up the messed up qgroups on subvol deletion.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>   fs/btrfs/qgroup.c | 15 +++++++++++++--
>   1 file changed, 13 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> index 96746ca19e17..a93d49015fc7 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> @@ -4968,8 +4968,19 @@ int btrfs_record_squota_delta(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
>   	list_for_each_entry(qg, &qgroup_list, iterator) {
>   		struct btrfs_qgroup_list *glist;
>   
> -		qg->excl += num_bytes * sign;
> -		qg->rfer += num_bytes * sign;
> +		ASSERT(qg->excl == qg->rfer);
> +		if (sign < 0 && qg->excl < num_bytes) {
> +			btrfs_warn(fs_info,
> +				   "squota underflow qg %hu/%llu excl %llu num_bytes %llu",
> +				   btrfs_qgroup_level(qg->qgroupid),
> +				   btrfs_qgroup_subvolid(qg->qgroupid),
> +				   qg->excl, num_bytes);

This may not be noisy enough for fstests to catch.

Maybe a WARN_ON_ONCE()?

Thanks,
Qu

> +			qg->excl = 0;
> +			qg->rfer = 0;
> +		} else {
> +			qg->excl += num_bytes * sign;
> +			qg->rfer += num_bytes * sign;
> +		}
>   		qgroup_dirty(fs_info, qg);
>   
>   		list_for_each_entry(glist, &qg->groups, next_group)

On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 12:34:52PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> A recent report of read-only filesystems since enabling squotas:
> 
> https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/adnBhWfJQ1n3hZC8@merlins.org/
> 
> led me to investigate a handful of issues in squotas and find some trivial and
> not so trivial bugs in their lifetime management. The aim of the series is to
> strengthen enforcement of the invariant: "a squota qgroup must outlast extents
> that are accounted to it".
> 
> This series consists of two fixes to squota lifetime bugs and two patches that
> reduce the consequences of errors in lifetime management from full fs goes
> read-only abort to "warning and inaccurate squotas which can be deleted
> eventually"
> 
> Additional improvements to btrfs check and fstests to follow.
> 
> Boris Burkov (4):
>   btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup
>   btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation
>   btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow
>   btrfs: swallow btrfs_record_squota_delta() enoent

Looks good to me, as much I can understand it. Please add it to
for-next.
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-12 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH 1/4] btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch fixes a bug in the Btrfs file system where deleting a subvolume quota (squota) group could break an invariant, leading to inconsistent state. The fix unifies the deletion check logic for both full qgroups and squotas, ensuring that the correct checks are performed before deleting a subvolume quota group. This prevents potential issues with shared extents outliving their owning subvolumes. The patch modifies the `can_delete_parent_qgroup` function to perform additional checks specific to squota groups, including verifying that there is no root and checking the usage of the parent qgroup.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-12 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH 2/4] btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch fixes a problem in Btrfs where quota accounting is incorrect when enabling quotas for the first time. When the first transaction that enables quotas completes, it sets a flag to indicate that quotas are enabled, but this can cause a deadlock and lead to incorrect quota values. The patch introduces two changes: first, it sets the enable generation to the next transaction's ID instead of the current one, ensuring that extents written during the enabling transaction are not accounted for; second, it adds a new flag to record squota deltas for extents written between the end of the enabling transaction and when the quotas-enabled flag is set. This fixes an underflow issue where metadata tree blocks would be freed prematurely, causing mismatched usage errors in btrfs check.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-12 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow Neutral LLM

This patch series addresses quota accounting issues in Btrfs, specifically a problem where metadata tree block allocations can be undercounted in certain scenarios, leading to qgroups becoming permanently orphaned after subvolume deletion. The patch introduces clamping of rfer and excl values to zero on underflow to mitigate this issue.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-12 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH 4/4] btrfs: swallow btrfs_record_squota_delta() enoent Neutral LLM

This patch series aims to harden the squota deletion mechanism in Btrfs, making it impossible for an extent to be accounted to a qgroup that outlives its qgroup. The author has re-considered their previous stance on this issue due to recent bugs and believes adding extra protection is necessary. The patch modifies the btrfs_record_squota_delta() function to swallow ENOENT errors, effectively masking real bugs.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Qu Wenruo ↳ replying to Boris Burkov Needs Work LLM
Requested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE to catch real bugs in debug builds, rather than masking them by swallowing ENOENT errors.
Show original comment 

在 2026/5/13 05:04, Boris Burkov 写道:
> I thought that it was likely I could harden squota deletion to the point
> that it was impossible to end up with an extent accounted to a qgroup
> outliving its qgroup. Several recent bugs have made me re-consider that
> position.
> 
> Ultimately, this is a tradeoff between short term stability and long
> term strictness, but I think given that there could be another layer of
> bugs behind the 2-3 I just fixed, I would feel much more confident in
> people using squotas if the risk was "your values can get a bit out of
> whack which you can fix by deleting stuff or
> disabling/re-enabling/repairing" vs "it will abort your filesystem".
> 
> As the final nail in the coffin, the Meta production kernel was lacking
> earlier fixes from me and Qu regarding subvol qgroup lifetime, so this
> is what we have been testing at scale, so I think at least for now
> upstream should have the same extra layer of protection.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>   fs/btrfs/qgroup.c | 2 +-
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> index a93d49015fc7..f9e5005028dc 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> @@ -4959,7 +4959,7 @@ int btrfs_record_squota_delta(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
>   	spin_lock(&fs_info->qgroup_lock);
>   	qgroup = find_qgroup_rb(fs_info, root);
>   	if (!qgroup) {
> -		ret = -ENOENT;
> +		ret = 0;

I appreciate that end users won't be bothered with transaction abort, 
but on the other hand, this seems to mask real bugs.

Can we add a WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BTRFS_DEBUG))) so that we 
can catch such problems for debug builds?

Thanks,
Qu>   		goto out;
>   	}
>

Last comment 2026-05-14 [PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests Neutral LLM

Show original comment 
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 05:43:33PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> The enable_quota helper in btrfs/301 takes a mode argument ('n', 's',
> or anything else) to switch between "no quota", "simple quota", and
> "full quota" enable.  Only the simple-quota path is ever exercised by
> this test, so the dispatch is dead weight and the indirection just
> obscures the actual btrfs command being run.
> 
> Inline the two call sites to call 'btrfs quota enable --simple'
> directly and drop the helper.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---

Hahah, to be honest, the title '[PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests' gave me
a minor heart attack. I thought it was a critical bug report about fstests
accidentally deleting itself while running :-D

>  tests/btrfs/301 | 14 ++------------
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/tests/btrfs/301 b/tests/btrfs/301
> index 1f72a97b..31243aab 100755
> --- a/tests/btrfs/301
> +++ b/tests/btrfs/301
> @@ -169,16 +169,6 @@ do_enospc_falloc()
>  	do_falloc $file $sz
>  }
>  
> -enable_quota()
> -{
> -	local mode=$1
> -
> -	[ $mode == "n" ] && return
> -	arg=$([ $mode == "s" ] && echo "--simple")
> -
> -	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable $arg $SCRATCH_MNT
> -}
> -
>  get_subvid()
>  {
>  	_btrfs_get_subvolid $SCRATCH_MNT subv
> @@ -198,7 +188,7 @@ prepare()
>  {
>  	_scratch_mkfs >> $seqres.full
>  	_scratch_mount
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT
>  	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG subvolume create $subv >> $seqres.full
>  	local subvid=$(get_subvid)
>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
> @@ -421,7 +411,7 @@ enable_mature()
>  	# Sync before enabling squotas to reliably *not* count the writes
>  	# we did before enabling.
>  	sync
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT

This change doesn't seem related to patch 2/2. I think we can review
these 2 patches separately.

For this patch, I'm fine with or without it. If the btrfs list prefers
to have it, or move it to be a common helper, I'm good with that.

Thanks,
Zorro

>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
>  	_scratch_cycle_mount
>  	usage=$(get_subvol_usage $subvid)
> --

[PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to cover various race conditions between qgroup deletion and squota writes. The tests aim to identify potential bugs in the handling of extent ownership references (EXTENT_OWNER_REF) when a subvolume is deleted or its qgroup is destroyed. The scenarios tested include freeing an extent whose owner qgroup is gone, creating an extent while the owning subvolume is being deleted, destroying a live subvolume's qgroup with delayed refs, and destroying a qgroup belonging to a still-mounted subvolume. The patch ensures that these operations are properly handled by the Btrfs kernel to prevent underflows or transaction aborts.

Discussions / RFCs (0)
No activity
Reviews Given (9)
Last comment 2026-05-11 Re: [PATCH] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock in find_first_inode_to_shrink() Neutral LLM

Patch aims to drop root->inodes lock in find_first_inode_to_shrink() but raises questions about scope and potential follow-up changes.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Boris Burkov Neutral LLM
Agreed with the patch's intention but suggested considering broader implications, including potential changes to cond_resched_lock() and other lock holders.
Signals: considering broader implications
Filipe Manana ↳ replying to Boris Burkov Neutral LLM
Clarified that the patch should focus on root->inodes.xa_lock and not address other locks, suggesting a more targeted approach.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 4:33 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
>
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 01:37:05PM +0100, Filipe Manana wrote:
> > On Sun, May 10, 2026 at 6:46 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
> > >
> > > find_first_inode_to_shrink() locks root->inodes, then loops over them,
> > > skipping those where it can't lock the extent_tree, or which have no
> > > extent_maps or it can't igrab. When it doesn't pick an inode, it
> > > attempts to share the cpu/lock with cond_resched_lock().
> > >
> > > However, that has a subtle problem associated with it.
> > > cond_resched_lock() only drops the lock if it needs to actually call
> > > schedule(). With CONFIG_PREEMPT_NONE, this means the full timeslice as
> > > detected at ticks. With 8+ cpus and default tunables, this is 2.8ms. So
> > > regardless of HZ, we will run for at least 2.8ms in this loop without
> > > dropping the lock, assuming it finds no suitable inodes. If HZ is
> > > small enough, it might be even worse as the tick granularity becomes
> > > bigger than the timeslice.
> > >
> > > The knock-on effect of this is that callers to
> > > btrfs_del_inode_from_root() like kswapd trying to shrink the inode slab
> > > or userspace threads calling evict() will spin on xa_lock(&root->inodes)
> > > for 2.8ms, so the extent map shrinker dominates the lock even though
> > > ostensibly it is intending to share it. This produces memory pressure as
> > > there is only one kswapd and it runs sequentially so it can get stuck in
> > > the inode slab shrinking.
> > >
> > > To fix it, simply replace cond_resched_lock() with an open coded variant
> > > which unconditionally does unlock/lock around cond_resched. Sharing the
> > > lock is decoupled from sharing the cpu, and all the users of the lock
> > > now share it fairly.
> > >
> > > I was able to reproduce this on test systems by producing a lot of empty
> > > files (to make a big root->inodes xarray), then producing memory
> > > pressure by reading large files larger than ram, triggering kswapd and
> > > the extent_map shrinker. The lock contention is visible with perf or
> > > lockstat. This patch also relieved a user-apparent bottleneck on a
> > > production system from the original report.
> > >
> > > Tested-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> > > Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> > > ---
> > >  fs/btrfs/extent_map.c | 4 +++-
> > >  1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> > >
> > > diff --git a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> > > index 514d7b59cbe5..fce9c5cc0122 100644
> > > --- a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> > > +++ b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> > > @@ -1246,7 +1246,9 @@ static struct btrfs_inode *find_first_inode_to_shrink(struct btrfs_root *root,
> > >                 write_unlock(&tree->lock);
> > >  next:
> > >                 from = btrfs_ino(inode) + 1;
> > > -               cond_resched_lock(&root->inodes.xa_lock);
> > > +               xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> > > +               cond_resched();
> > > +               xa_lock(&root->inodes);
> >
> > We call cond_resched_lock() in other places, including
> > btrfs_find_first_inode(), which uses the same lock.
> > Why do that only here?
> >
> > Thanks.
>
> Good point. I was just narrowly following the profile and what I managed
> to reproduce directly.
>
> I did consider whether it made sense to change cond_resched_lock()
> itself but decided to do just this to try out the idea to start.
>
> I will definitely change btrfs_find_first_inode() in v2. Would you like
> me to also get rid of the other uses of cond_resched_lock() from btrfs,
> assuming it makes similar sense at each callsite?

I think this patch should only address the lock root->inodes.xa_lock
(all the places that call cond_resched_lock() for it).
And then other patches for other locks.

Thanks.

>
> >
> >
> > >         }
> > >         xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> > >
> > > --
> > > 2.54.0
> > >
> > >
Signals: targeted approach

Dmitry Ilvokhin

11 items
Patches Submitted (8)
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long a lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

6 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Steven Rostedt Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not be accurate for reader/writer locks, as it fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, which could lead to spurious events. The reviewer suggested rephrasing the patch description to use more descriptive language, specifically recommending against using 'next commit' and instead suggesting a more detailed explanation of the code refactoring.

  queued_spin_unlock(), refactor the code to allow out of line calls when
  the tracepoint is enabled.

Or something like that.

-- Steve

Signals: uncertainty, overhead
Peter Zijlstra Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Peter Zijlstra noted that the patch adds unnecessary instructions (12-15 bytes) to the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the original MOV instruction, which he considers 'quite ludicrous'. He also disagreed with the assumption that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size on x86, stating that the unlock is smaller than a function call.
Show original comment 
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.
View on lore ↗
Signals: binary size concerns, unnecessary instructions
Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH v6 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read() Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified, and the slowpath is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 ⏱ No comments yet [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v6 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release() Neutral LLM

This patch introduces a new function, queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It refactors the existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to use this new function. This change has no functional impact and is a preparatory step for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock().

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention and release events. The patch uses an arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path, minimizing performance impact. When enabled, the contention check, trace call, and unlock are combined in an out-of-line function to further minimize hot path impact. The patch also includes estimates of binary size increases due to this change.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants

Reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already unlocked, and suggested adding a check to ensure the lock is locked before tracing. The reviewer suggested removing a phrase from the patch description that is considered unnecessary in Git change logs. Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and may cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of returning immediately, the function should be called directly The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out a redundant call to queued_spin_release() in the code and requested its removal.

Ongoing Submitted 2026-05-05 Last comment 2026-05-13 ⏱ No recent activity [PATCH v6 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock() Neutral LLM

On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:36 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Extend the contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, using the
> same out-of-line traced unlock approach as queued spinlocks.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> ---
>  include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h | 22 ++++++++++++++++++++++
>  kernel/locking/qrwlock.c      | 16 ++++++++++++++++
>  2 files changed, 38 insertions(+)
> 
> diff --git a/include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h b/include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h
> index 4b627bafba8b..274c19006125 100644
> --- a/include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h
> +++ b/include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h
> @@ -14,6 +14,7 @@
>  #define __ASM_GENERIC_QRWLOCK_H
>  
>  #include <linux/atomic.h>
> +#include <linux/tracepoint-defs.h>
>  #include <asm/barrier.h>
>  #include <asm/processor.h>
>  
> @@ -35,6 +36,10 @@
>   */
>  extern void queued_read_lock_slowpath(struct qrwlock *lock);
>  extern void queued_write_lock_slowpath(struct qrwlock *lock);
> +extern void queued_read_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock);
> +extern void queued_write_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock);
> +
> +DECLARE_TRACEPOINT(contended_release);
>  
>  /**
>   * queued_read_trylock - try to acquire read lock of a queued rwlock
> @@ -115,6 +120,17 @@ static __always_inline void __queued_read_unlock(struct qrwlock *lock)
>   */
>  static inline void queued_read_unlock(struct qrwlock *lock)
>  {
> +	/*
> +	 * Trace and unlock are combined in the traced unlock variant so
> +	 * the compiler does not need to preserve the lock pointer across
> +	 * the function call, avoiding callee-saved register save/restore
> +	 * on the hot path.
> +	 */
> +	if (tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)) {
> +		queued_read_unlock_traced(lock);

Same issue here about duplicating the code.
> +		return;
> +	}
> +
>  	__queued_read_unlock(lock);
>  }
>  
> @@ -129,6 +145,12 @@ static __always_inline void __queued_write_unlock(struct qrwlock *lock)
>   */
>  static inline void queued_write_unlock(struct qrwlock *lock)
>  {
> +	/* See comment in queued_read_unlock(). */
> +	if (tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)) {
> +		queued_write_unlock_traced(lock);

And here.

> +		return;
> +	}
> +
>  	__queued_write_unlock(lock);
>  }
>  
> diff --git a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> index d2ef312a8611..5ae4b0372719 100644
> --- a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> +++ b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> @@ -90,3 +90,19 @@ void __lockfunc queued_write_lock_slowpath(struct qrwlock *lock)
>  	trace_contention_end(lock, 0);
>  }
>  EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_write_lock_slowpath);
> +
> +void __lockfunc queued_read_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock)
> +{
> +	if (queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock))
> +		trace_call__contended_release(lock);

Just have this trace and not actually do any locking.


> +	__queued_read_unlock(lock);
> +}
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_read_unlock_traced);
> +
> +void __lockfunc queued_write_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock)
> +{
> +	if (queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock))
> +		trace_call__contended_release(lock);

Ditto.

-- Steve

> +	__queued_write_unlock(lock);
> +}
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_write_unlock_traced);
Signals: duplicated code concerns
Discussions / RFCs (0)
No activity
Reviews Given (3)
Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Last comment 2026-05-14 Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock Neutral LLM

The patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock, but the current design has duplication and inefficiency.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Dmitry Ilvokhin Neutral LLM
Acknowledged a suggestion to combine tracepoint and unlock in the same function, but noted that this introduces duplication and inefficiency.
Signals: NEEDS_WORK
Steven Rostedt ↳ replying to Dmitry Ilvokhin Neutral LLM
Agreed that the current design has duplication and suggested adding more comments to explain the need for updating other locations if changes are made here.
Show original comment 
On Thu, 14 May 2026 14:13:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> > > +void __lockfunc queued_spin_release_traced(struct qspinlock *lock)
> > > +{
> > > +	if (queued_spin_is_contended(lock))
> > > +		trace_call__contended_release(lock);
> > > +	queued_spin_release(lock);  
> > 
> > And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.  
> 
> This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
> Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
> with GCC 11).
> 
> Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
>   
>     endbr64
>     xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
>     movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
>     decl %gs:__preempt_count
>     je   preempt
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
>     call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
>     jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
>     call __SCT__preempt_schedule
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
> 
> With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
>   
>     endbr64
>     push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
>     mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
>     xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
>     movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
>     decl %gs:__preempt_count
>     je   preempt
>     pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
>     call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
>     jmp  unlock                      ; cold
>     call __SCT__preempt_schedule
>     pop  %rbx
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
> 
> Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
> all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
> combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about
needing to update the other location if anything changes here? And perhaps
comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve
Signals: NEEDS_WORK
Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Acks / Tags Given (0)
No activity

Gregory Price

41 items
Patches Submitted (1)
[RFC PATCH 0/2] virtio-balloon: extended stats and push mode 2 patches
Show 2 individual patches
  • [1/2] virtio-balloon: extend stats with memory composition and pressure data
  • [2/2] virtio-balloon: add stats push mode
Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies

This patch series extends the virtio-balloon stats virtqueue to include new tags for memory composition and pressure data, providing more accurate reclaimable estimates and balloon sizing information. It also adds an optional push mode for guest-initiated stat reporting, allowing hosts to configure guests to push stats at a guaranteed cadence without waiting for host confirmation.

Discussions / RFCs (1)
[RFC] __GFP_UNMAPPED and __GFP_PRIVATE follow up Neutral llm-chunked-no-replies
Reviews Given (29)
Last comment 2026-05-15 Re: [RFC] __GFP_UNMAPPED and __GFP_PRIVATE follow up Neutral LLM

Proposal to introduce new GFP flags __GFP_UNMAPPED and __GFP_PRIVATE, and discussion around their implementation and testing.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Gregory Price Needs Work LLM
Raised concerns about the complexity of replacing global NODE_DATA with a new interface and suggested using alloc_context to extend existing functionality.
Signals: CONCERN
Brendan Jackman ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Positive LLM
Clarified the intention to use alloc_context and suggested carving out a distinct node data instance for testing purposes.
Show original comment 
On Fri May 15, 2026 at 3:48 PM UTC, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Fri, May 15, 2026 at 09:43:02AM +0000, Brendan Jackman wrote:
>> 
>> Yeah, I have had a similar thought before. In fact, I wonder if we could
>> have a pointer in there that effectively allows you to replace
>> NODE_DATA? I think that would be a more general mechanism to achieve
>> that `managed_node` thing?
>>
>
> Well, alloc_context already contains a nodemask.  I could see even
> pulling that argument into the struct if we seriously consider exporting
> alloc_context.
>
> I'll have to think about the NODE_DATA replacement.  I don't know if
> that's really feasible consider that this structure is used statically
> all over the kernel for runtime node-data lookups from pages/folios.

Oh yeah I wasn't thinking of replacing it completely, more that the
global NODE_DATA is the "default" node data (and the pointer field being
NULL would probably mean to use that), but then private node datas could
also exist.

>> My original motive for that was: if we could get the allocator to stop
>> [unconditionally] mutating global variables it would make it easier to
>> test.
>> 
>
> Can you expand on this a bit more?
> What globals are you referred to exactly?

IIRC it's just a few, the vast majority of the global mutable state is
ultimately downstream of NODE_DATA, plus there are some tuning knobs.

> There has been a desire on our side (Meta) to make mm/ more testable in
> general (for both performance and correctness) - include page_alloc.c
>
> But with everything so tightly coupled the best we can presently do is
> runtime testing of benchmarks and workloads.
>
> The same issue exists for things like LRU/MGLRU, where you can't really
> isolate a change because you get emergent properties.

Yeah basically just this; if you try to write a test that constructs
some specific condition to try and hit the nastiest possible fallback
case, you are racing against the real system. Ditto if you wanna check
your code against page_group_by_mobility_disabled=1, well you'd better
figure out a way to actually boot a whole system with that property.

I spent a few days trying to solve this in the way that VMA and xarray
etc have, i.e. by making the page allocator a library and then testing
it from userspace. I think that would work but it needs much more than a
few days to make it happen (admittedly, I had tried to do this with AI
at the time and it failed miserably. Maybe with today's models it would
work, which could massively accelerate the grunt work of e.g. splitting
stuff into new files).

But anyway, if you could carve out a distinct node data etc you could just
write KUnit tests that operate on a completely isolated "instance" of
the allocator, even though it still runs in a real kernel.

Signals: APPROVAL
Last comment 2026-05-13 Re: [PATCH v2 00/22] mm: Add __GFP_UNMAPPED Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series introduces a new flag __GFP_UNMAPPED to the Linux kernel's memory allocation system, allowing for more efficient handling of unmapped memory allocations. The current approach involves adding an additional level of indirection, increasing the migratetype matrix. An alternative solution proposed by Gregory Price is to have the buddy unmap memory after zeroing and allocation, and remap it on free. This would simplify the process and hide the changes from existing users.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Brendan Jackman ↳ replying to Gregory Price Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that the current design only supports one migratetype being unmapped, and requested a more general solution that can handle arbitrary migratetypes. Reviewer noted that the proposed __GFP_UNMAPPED mechanism may not be universally applicable due to the presence of guest memory, where a user address is not available for write() operations. Reviewer noted that the proposed __GFP_UNMAPPED mechanism is not primarily about default memory state, but rather about batching and reducing TLB flushes during allocation; they suggested unmap-on-alloc approach as a cleaner alternative to achieve this goal. Reviewer noted that doing unmapping on a per-allocation basis would result in most map-unmap cycles being pointless, as most pages are expected to be unmapped, and suggested considering the opposite mechanism of unmap-on-free instead.
Show original comment 
Because the fact that only one migratetype currently supports being
unmapped is a temporary happenstance of the guest_memfd usecase. In
general, this needs to support having unmapped variants of ~arbitrary
migratetypes.

---

Yeah, I actually only noticed that yesterday due to your posts on that
thread! I need to investigate it further. My assumption has always been
that this isn't a general solution because we don't always _have_ a user
address (e.g. for guest_memfd it's important that we can populate the
memory via write(), so there's no user address), but it's pretty likely
I'm missing something there.

---

That would be cleaner indeed, but the key question here isn't about the
default state of memory here, it's about batching.

The reason we need to do it at the block granularity is that a TLB flush
every time we allocate one of these pages is a performance nonstarter -
that's actually the entire point of this series. If you can afford a TLB
flush per allocation then you don't need __GFP_UNMAPPED for the
guest_memfd usecase, the existing direct map removal series [0] is
already fine.

[0] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410151746.61150-1-kalyazin@amazon.com/

---

Hopefully my prev paragraph explained that it's not viable anyway, but:
if we _did_ do the [un]mapping on a per-allocation basis, the
disadvantage of unmap-on-alloc is just that we expect most pages in the
system to be unmapped. So the majority of map-unmap cycles are pointless
(map on free, but we're probably gonna unmap again on allloc).

Cheers,
Brendan
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, generalization needed, requested investigation
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Brendan Jackman Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the stale TLB entry issue might not be related to the __GFP_UNMAPPED patch, but rather a problem of incorrect TLB flushing in page freeing or unmapping. Author plans to spend more time understanding the issue. Author acknowledges reviewer's preference for block-granularity map/unmap operations and asks for cost data on page vs block operations, indicating a need for further revision to optimize the approach.
Show original comment 
Hm.  I'm not quite wrapping my head around the TLB issue fully.

If there's no kernel direct mapping, and there's no userland mapping,
the stale TLB entry comes from... the page formerly being present in the
page tables and a stale TLB entry lying about after the page is freed?

If that's the case, that sounds more like someone isn't flushing the TLB
entry correctly when the page is freed or unmapped (for a transient
mermap situation), rather than an issue to be handled by the allocator.

I think I just need to spend a little more time understanding the TLB
issue.

---

That tracks.  So whatever the case, it seems like you prefer
block-granularity map/unmap operations, rather than per-allocation.

Do you have numbers on what the cost of map/unmap on a page vs block is?

Whatever the case is - you're pushing that cost onto *someone*, so
having that data would at least let us know the value of pushing most
memory to be unmapped by default rather than the opposite.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledging a potential misunderstanding, requesting more time for analysis, acknowledges reviewer's preference
Vlastimil (SUSE) ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Neutral LLM
Reviewer noted that the direct mapping is assumed to be always present and unchanged, and only the kernel can access its contents; they questioned whether this assumption holds true in all scenarios, specifically mentioning UAF bugs or CPU bugs like Meltdown.
Show original comment 
It's the direct mapping, we assume it's always there and unchanged, and only
kernel can access the contents through it. So nobody flushes it when freeing
any pages. Userspace processes can't exploit anything stale there, in
absence of kernel's UAF bugs (or e.g. Meltdown like cpu bugs).
View on lore ↗
Signals: questioning, raising concerns
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Vlastimil (SUSE) Neutral LLM
Author addressed concerns about adding a new level of indirection (GFP flags) instead of using existing mechanisms, proposed an alternative approach by factoring out alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() and introducing alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof(), but also suggested considering a more radical change to the buddy interface with context-dependent allocation functions. No clear resolution or fix planned.
Show original comment 
Ah, I follow.

If everything is default-unmapped, then you don't have to worry about
this issue - except when a stolen block is returned or an ephemeral
mapping is unmapped after the operation.

pivoting...

On the GFP front, i wonder if you could factor out the core of
alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() and add alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof()
which adds (alloc_flags |= ALLOC_UNMAPPED) instead of adding
__GFP_UNMAPPED.

I have been considering something similar for __GFP_PRIVATE, but this
has the added downside of increasing the surface of the buddy for each
new narrow use case (in my case, private nodes, in this case unmapped
allocations).

unless of course we nip that in the bud with something like

struct page *
alloc_pages_special(enum buddy_context ctxt, gfp_t gfp_mask, ...)
{
    switch (ctxt) {
       ... internal-only details about how that case is handled ...
    }
}

and just go ahead and allow the buddy to grow internally without adding
new gfp flags or an infinite number of interfaces.

Of course that means users have to know the context in which they're
being allocated.  Right now you can kind of "transiently cheat" by
passing a GFP flag through a bunch of interfaces and that makes certain
allocations reachable - but maybe we should not be encouraging that kind
of design for these kinds of allocator extensions?

Just spitballing here on the GFP issue since that seems to be coming to
a head on multiple fronts.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: considering alternative approaches, suggesting radical changes

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Gregory Price Neutral LLM
Raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address. Asked for clarification on whether this is correct on all architectures.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:59:23PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:52:10PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:55:40AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:37:37AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:01:55AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > > 
> > > > > +/*
> > > > > + * Sentinel for user_addr: indicates a non-user allocation.
> > > > > + * Cannot use 0 because address 0 is a valid userspace mapping.
> > > > > + */
> > > > > +#define USER_ADDR_NONE	((unsigned long)-1)
> > > > 
> > > > Ehm, hm.  Does -1 hold as a non-user address across all architectures?
> > > > 
> > > > What about in linear addressing / no VM mode?
> > > 
> > > this is used on a fault. I don't think there are any faults then?
> > > But maybe FAULT_ADDR_NONE would be clearer.
> > >
> > 
> > Meh, naming here is less relevant than the sentinel correctness.
> > 
> > My only concern is really whether -1 could end up being a valid address
> > in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.
> > 
> > Is why I asked about whether this is correct on all architectures.
> 
> I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
> page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
> enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.
> 
>   if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
>       return -ENOMEM;
> 
> 
> So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
> On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
> never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
> VMA.
> 

That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory
Signals: concerns about sentinel correctness, potential issue with -1
Michael S. Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Positive LLM
Clarified that the last page of the address space is never a valid user mapping, and that -1 can never be within any VMA due to kernel enforcement.
Signals: clarification on sentinel correctness
Gregory Price ↳ replying to Michael S. Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Agreed that the clarification is sufficient, but suggested documenting it in the changelog or comment.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:47:48PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:36:59PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > 
> > This feels like a very odd pattern:
> > 
> >   1) ask for __GFP_ZERO
> >   2) Have to check whether it was actually zeroed
> > 
> > Seems like the zeroing piece should just be sunk in if you're going to
> > ask for __GFP_ZERO anyway.  And in that case, maybe just `bool zero` as
> > an argument, rather than GFP (to avoid future overloading of flags).
> > 
> > ~Gregory
> 
> Heh. The reason is that it either allocates from buddy - using gfp flags
> or from the pool, in which case it zeroes.
> 
> We could even avoid the bool - just test __GFP_ZERO inside
> alloc_hugetlb_folio. Would that be better?
> 

Hard to know until we see the full shape of things, but it seems
reasonable if we can eliminate one or both new arguments that this would
be a good thing and the logic should just be sunk into hugetlb.

~Gregory
Signals: suggestion for documentation

Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Author responded to a suggestion from Michael Tsirkin that instead of passing the was_reported flag, they should take a temporary copy of all page flags and pass them in. Author sarcastically referenced a previous instance where this approach was taken.

I mean yes, reported and zeroed) Just zeroed does not exist at this

View on lore ↗
Signals: inconsistency, stage-specific
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Author is addressing feedback about passing page flags through the buddy split process, suggesting it might be more efficient to pass them all at once and let expand() handle checking instead of individually passing each flag.
Show original comment 
More of a forward looking question: if we already know we're adding
was_reported and was_zeroed, maybe we should just pass the page flags
through entirely and let expand() check them instead of passing it
through individually.

We end up doing something similar with post_alloc_hook later as well, so
I'm wondering if there's a general improvement that can be made here.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: clarifying question, exploring alternative approach
Michael Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer Michael Tsirkin asked if the patch should use a bitwise operation for type safety instead of a long, and requested clarification on the desired approach.
Show original comment 
sure, will do. do you want a bitwise thing for type safety? or just a
long?
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the patch needs to handle PG_reported and PG_zeroed flags in the expand function, suggested a possible implementation using stable_page_flags(page) and test_bit() macros.
Show original comment 
Can probably just do like:

flags = stable_page_flags(page);

plumbing...

expand(..., buddy_flags) {
	if (test_bit(PG_reported, buddy_flags))
		...
	if (test_bit(PG_zeroed, buddy_flags))
		...
}

or whatever makes sense.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged fix needed

Last comment 2026-05-11 Re: [PATCH resend v6 03/30] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch is about improving the handling of user addresses in memory allocation, specifically to make cache-friendly zeroing more efficient. The current approach involves passing USER_ADDR_NONE through various functions, but this can be simplified by introducing a separate function for user-land bound allocations. This change aims to reduce code churn and potential subtle corruption issues related to cache flushing.

4 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Michael Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer questioned the use of user_addr in this patch, suggesting it may not be necessary since faults are rare and proposing an alternative constant FAULT_ADDR_NONE for clarity.
Show original comment 
this is used on a fault. I don't think there are any faults then?
But maybe FAULT_ADDR_NONE would be clearer.
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested changes, proposed alternative
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Author acknowledged that the concern about -1 being a valid user address across all architectures is still open, and is seeking clarification on this point. Author acknowledged the reviewer's suggestion to prefer stack style changelog formatting and agreed that spelling out the allocation process is a good approach.
Show original comment 
Meh, naming here is less relevant than the sentinel correctness.

My only concern is really whether -1 could end up being a valid address
in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.

Is why I asked about whether this is correct on all architectures.

---

Yeah simply spelling it out, not asking for a change.  Probably no
better way to go about it.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: clarification sought, no clear resolution, acknowledgment
Michael Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Neutral LLM
Reviewer questioned whether -1 is a valid user address across all architectures, noting that the kernel enforces this by not creating mappings whose end wraps past -1.
Show original comment 
I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.

  if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
      return -ENOMEM;


So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
VMA.
View on lore ↗
Signals: questioning, clarification
Gregory Price (author) ↳ replying to Michael Tsirkin Positive LLM
Author acknowledged that the preference for stack style documentation should be noted in the changelog or code comments, and agreed to add it.
Show original comment 
That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory
View on lore ↗
Signals: acknowledged feedback, agreed to add documentation
Last comment 2026-05-11 Re: [PATCH resend v6 03/30] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing Neutral LLM

The patch aims to thread user_addr through the page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing, but a reviewer raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants

Raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address. Asked for clarification on whether this is correct on all architectures.

> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:52:10PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:

> > > > 
> > > > > +/*
> > > > > + * Sentinel for user_addr: indicates a non-user allocation.
> > > > > + * Cannot use 0 because address 0 is a valid userspace mapping.
> > > > > + */

> > > >

> > > >

> > >

> >

> > in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.

> 
> I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
> page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
> enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.
> 
>   if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
>       return -ENOMEM;
> 
> 
> So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
> On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
> never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
> VMA.
> 

That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory

> > On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 11:34:14AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:

> > > > > > 
> > > > > > Maybe mildly out of scope but worth asking:  Are there other flags that
> > > > > > should be retained/propogated on a split?  If so, rather than pass

> > > >

> sure, will do. do you want a bitwise thing for type safety? or just a
> long?
>

Can probably just do like:

flags = stable_page_flags(page);

plumbing...

expand(..., buddy_flags) {
	if (test_bit(PG_reported, buddy_flags))
		...
	if (test_bit(PG_zeroed, buddy_flags))
		...
}

or whatever makes sense.

~Gregory
Signals: forward-looking question
Michael S. Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Positive LLM
Agreed to propagate PageReported flag and discussed potential bitwise operations for type safety.
Signals: agreement
Gregory Price ↳ replying to Michael S. Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Provided a possible implementation for propagating page flags using stable_page_flags() and test_bit().
Show original comment 
On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 02:00:31PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 09:49:33AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> 
> There are calls with no __GFP_ZERO but they do not allocate userspace pages.
> 
>   - drm_pagemap.c: GFP_HIGHUSER -- no zero. But this is a DRM device
>     page migration, the page content is preserved from the source.
> 
>   - test_hmm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. Test driver, pages get
>     content from device.
> 
>   - mm/ksm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. KSM merges identical
>     pages, content comes from the source page (copy).
> 
>   - mm/memory.c new_folio = GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE
>     - no zero. This is CoW, content is copied from old page.
> 
>   - mm/userfaultfd.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE - no zero. Content comes from userspace via userfaultfd.
> 
>   - arm64/fault.c: __GFP_ZEROTAGS not __GFP_ZERO. MTE tag zeroing, not page zeroing. Page is zeroed separately.
> 

Right, so in all of these cases, it would be just as correct to pass
USER_ADDR_NONE I imagine :]

i.e. the user address is irrelevant, and the caller is responsible for
     sanitization before return if it's relevant.

Otherwise, passing (user_addr != -1) the buddy takes care of it for you.

Just an obvious security bonus to all of this, but by no means a
requirement for your set.  Just an observation.

> 
> I'd do this on top if possible.
>

Yeah reasonable.

~Gregory
Signals: implementation suggestion
Michael S. Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Positive LLM
Acknowledged the proposed implementation and agreed on its clarity.
Signals: acknowledgment

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Gregory Price Neutral LLM

Last comment 2026-05-11 Re: [PATCH resend v6 02/30] mm: mempolicy: fix interleave index for unaligned VMA start Reviewed-by Neutral LLM

The patch aims to thread user_addr through the page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing, but a reviewer raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address.

3 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Gregory Price Neutral LLM
Raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address. Asked for clarification on whether this is correct on all architectures.
Show original comment 
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:59:23PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:52:10PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:55:40AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:37:37AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:01:55AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > > 
> > > > > +/*
> > > > > + * Sentinel for user_addr: indicates a non-user allocation.
> > > > > + * Cannot use 0 because address 0 is a valid userspace mapping.
> > > > > + */
> > > > > +#define USER_ADDR_NONE	((unsigned long)-1)
> > > > 
> > > > Ehm, hm.  Does -1 hold as a non-user address across all architectures?
> > > > 
> > > > What about in linear addressing / no VM mode?
> > > 
> > > this is used on a fault. I don't think there are any faults then?
> > > But maybe FAULT_ADDR_NONE would be clearer.
> > >
> > 
> > Meh, naming here is less relevant than the sentinel correctness.
> > 
> > My only concern is really whether -1 could end up being a valid address
> > in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.
> > 
> > Is why I asked about whether this is correct on all architectures.
> 
> I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
> page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
> enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.
> 
>   if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
>       return -ENOMEM;
> 
> 
> So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
> On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
> never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
> VMA.
> 

That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory
Signals: concerns about sentinel correctness, potential issue with -1
Michael S. Tsirkin ↳ replying to Gregory Price Positive LLM
Clarified that the last page of the address space is never a valid user mapping, and that -1 can never be within any VMA due to kernel enforcement.
Signals: clarification on sentinel correctness
Gregory Price ↳ replying to Michael S. Tsirkin Neutral LLM
Agreed that the clarification is sufficient, but suggested documenting it in the changelog or comment.
Signals: suggestion for documentation

Last comment 2026-05-14 [PATCH] exportfs: drop unused is_export parameter from xtab_read() and xtab_write() Neutral LLM

This patch removes the unused 'is_export' parameter from two functions, xtab_read() and xtab_write(), in the exportfs code. The parameter is always passed as 1, so it's unnecessary and can be simplified by eliminating dead code paths.

1 reviewer comment — 0 participants
0 participants
Jeff Layton Neutral LLM
The reviewer posted a follow-up reply to correct the subject line of the patch, which was incorrectly labeled as '[PATCH]'. The actual label should be '[nfs-utils PATCH]'.
Show original comment 
Oops, I meant to make this '[nfs-utils PATCH]'. Ahh well, anyway, it's
just a bit of cleanup...

-- Jeff


On Thu, 2026-05-14 at 15:40 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The is_export parameter is always passed as 1. Remove it and simplify
> both functions by eliminating the dead is_export == 0 code paths.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  support/export/xtab.c | 39 +++++++++++++--------------------------
>  1 file changed, 13 insertions(+), 26 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/support/export/xtab.c b/support/export/xtab.c
> index 282f15bc79cd..0a9660512b26 100644
> --- a/support/export/xtab.c
> +++ b/support/export/xtab.c
> @@ -33,11 +33,8 @@ int v4root_needed;
>  static void cond_rename(char *newfile, char *oldfile);
>  
>  static int
> -xtab_read(char *xtab, char *lockfn, int is_export)
> +xtab_read(char *xtab, char *lockfn)
>  {
> -    /* is_export == 0  => reading /proc/fs/nfs/exports - we know these things are exported to kernel
> -     * is_export == 1  => reading /var/lib/nfs/etab - these things are allowed to be exported
> -     */
>  	struct exportent	*xp;
>  	nfs_export		*exp;
>  	int			lockid;
> @@ -45,11 +42,10 @@ xtab_read(char *xtab, char *lockfn, int is_export)
>  	if ((lockid = xflock(lockfn, "r")) < 0)
>  		return 0;
>  	setexportent(xtab, "r");
> -	if (is_export == 1)
> -		v4root_needed = 1;
> -	while ((xp = getexportent(is_export==0)) != NULL) {
> -		if (!(exp = export_lookup(xp->e_hostname, xp->e_path, is_export != 1)) &&
> -		    !(exp = export_create(xp, is_export!=1))) {
> +	v4root_needed = 1;
> +	while ((xp = getexportent(0)) != NULL) {
> +		if (!(exp = export_lookup(xp->e_hostname, xp->e_path, 0)) &&
> +		    !(exp = export_create(xp, 0))) {
>                          if(xp->e_hostname) {
>                              free(xp->e_hostname);
>                              xp->e_hostname=NULL;
> @@ -60,17 +56,10 @@ xtab_read(char *xtab, char *lockfn, int is_export)
>                          }
>  			continue;
>  		}
> -		switch (is_export) {
> -		case 0:
> -			exp->m_exported = 1;
> -			break;
> -		case 1:
> -			exp->m_xtabent = 1;
> -			exp->m_mayexport = 1;
> -			if ((xp->e_flags & NFSEXP_FSID) && xp->e_fsid == 0)
> -				v4root_needed = 0;
> -			break;
> -		}  
> +		exp->m_xtabent = 1;
> +		exp->m_mayexport = 1;
> +		if ((xp->e_flags & NFSEXP_FSID) && xp->e_fsid == 0)
> +			v4root_needed = 0;
>                  if(xp->e_hostname) {
>                      free(xp->e_hostname);
>                      xp->e_hostname=NULL;
> @@ -90,7 +79,7 @@ xtab_read(char *xtab, char *lockfn, int is_export)
>  int
>  xtab_export_read(void)
>  {
> -	return xtab_read(etab.statefn, etab.lockfn, 1);
> +	return xtab_read(etab.statefn, etab.lockfn);
>  }
>  
>  /*
> @@ -100,7 +89,7 @@ xtab_export_read(void)
>   * fix the auth_reload logic as well...
>   */
>  static int
> -xtab_write(char *xtab, char *xtabtmp, char *lockfn, int is_export)
> +xtab_write(char *xtab, char *xtabtmp, char *lockfn)
>  {
>  	struct exportent	xe;
>  	nfs_export		*exp;
> @@ -114,9 +103,7 @@ xtab_write(char *xtab, char *xtabtmp, char *lockfn, int is_export)
>  
>  	for (i = 0; i < MCL_MAXTYPES; i++) {
>  		for (exp = exportlist[i].p_head; exp; exp = exp->m_next) {
> -			if (is_export && !exp->m_xtabent)
> -				continue;
> -			if (!is_export && ! exp->m_exported)
> +			if (!exp->m_xtabent)
>  				continue;
>  
>  			/* write out the export entry using the FQDN */
> @@ -137,7 +124,7 @@ xtab_write(char *xtab, char *xtabtmp, char *lockfn, int is_export)
>  int
>  xtab_export_write(void)
>  {
> -	return xtab_write(etab.statefn, etab.tmpfn, etab.lockfn, 1);
> +	return xtab_write(etab.statefn, etab.tmpfn, etab.lockfn);
>  }
>

Last comment 2026-05-16 [PATCH] filelock: move LEASE_BREAK_* flags out of #ifdef CONFIG_FILE_LOCKING Neutral LLM

This patch moves the LEASE_BREAK_* flags out of a conditional compilation block, making them available regardless of whether CONFIG_FILE_LOCKING is enabled or not. This change was necessary to fix a build break caused by the test robot's report.

Patches in the vfs-7.2.directory.delegations branch should appear in linux-next soon.

Please report any outstanding bugs that were missed during review in a
new review to the original patch series allowing us to drop it.

It's encouraged to provide Acked-bys and Reviewed-bys even though the
patch has now been applied. If possible patch trailers will be updated.

trailer updates or similar. If in doubt, please check the listed branch.

Last comment 2026-05-11 [PATCH v7 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE 3 patches
Show 3 individual patches
  • [1/3] mm: preserve PG_dropbehind flag during folio split Neutral David Hildenbrand (Arm)
  • [2/3] mm: track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback Neutral Last comment 2026-05-11 Christian Brauner Jeff Layton (author) David (Arm)
  • [3/3] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking Neutral Last comment 2026-05-11 Christian Brauner Jeff Layton (author)
Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch series improves write performance by addressing issues related to RWF_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of dirty pages. The patches fix integer promotion bugs on 32-bit systems, ensure proper stat accounting during cgroup writeback domain migration and folio split operations, and prevent use-after-free (UAF) errors in filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(). Additionally, the patches track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback, have the flusher write back a proportional number of pages after a DONTCACHE write, and include new benchmarks to test competing buffered and dontcache writers.

6 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
David (Arm) Acked-by Positive Heuristic
Gave Acked-by
View on lore ↗
Christian Brauner Neutral LLM
Christian Brauner expressed strong disagreement with AI-Assisted-by tags, considering them useless noise in the git log and suggested that they should be removed when applying patches, while advocating for crediting actual contributors.
Show original comment 
Picking up on something we discussed at LSFMM in one of the sessions as
an aside rant: I find these AI Assisted-by tags so useless tbh and just
pure noise in the git log _especially_ for a core developer like Jeff
that I really don't see the point of them and I'm always tempted to just
remove the tags when I apply. I have dropped them before because I found
them so pointless.

Crediting Jan here is the right thing to do and it provides actual value
and also just makes sure that a real person who spent time helping out
gets visibility in the git history. Why we should extend the same
courtesy to automated tooling is really beyond me. Somehow we've become
all convinced that these tools require a special status but have spent
months arguing about the usefulness of other tags.
View on lore ↗
Signals: disagreement, requested changes
Christian Brauner Inline Review Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
The reviewer pointed out a redundant check in the code, noting that test_and_set_bit() returns the old value of the bit, so the subsequent test_bit() call is unnecessary and can be removed. Reviewer Christian Brauner noted that the wb_get() call is unnecessary because unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin() already holds a reference to wb, and requested that it be removed.
Show original comment 
Doesn't test_and_set_bit() return the old value? IOW, if it sees that
WB_start_dontcache was already set it'll return true? So you can remove
the test_bit() call, right?

---

void filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(struct address_space *mapping)
{
	struct inode *inode = mapping->host;
	struct bdi_writeback *wb;
	struct wb_lock_cookie cookie = {};

	wb = unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin(inode, &cookie);
	if (wb_has_dirty_io(wb) && !test_and_set_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state))
		wb_get(wb);
	else
		wb = NULL;
	unlocked_inode_to_wb_end(inode, &cookie);

	if (wb) {
		wb_wakeup(wb);
		wb_put(wb);
	}
}

No?
View on lore ↗
Signals: requested correction, implied minor improvement, unnecessary reference
Jeff Layton (author) ↳ replying to Christian Brauner Neutral LLM
The author acknowledges that adding Assisted-by tags is a matter of fairness, but expresses uncertainty about their usefulness and whether they should be added at all.
Show original comment 
To be clear, Christoph and Ritesh also contributed a lot of review and
suggestions.

I was mainly trying to follow this new verbiage in
Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst:

------------------8<-------------------
Using Assisted-by:
------------------
If you used any sort of advanced coding tool in the creation of your patch,
you need to acknowledge that use by adding an Assisted-by tag.  Failure to
do so may impede the acceptance of your work.  Please see
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst for details regarding the
acknowledgment of coding assistants.
------------------8<-------------------

If we're demanding this from anyone, then we should demand it from
everyone. I don't think we want one set of rules for core contributors
and another set for other folks.

As to whether we should add them at all -- I don't know. I think it
really comes down to what we intend to do with this info. I'll play
devil's advocate for the moment:

The cost of adding these tags is low. It's just a few extra bits in the
repo. Maybe this could eventually have historical value?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Last comment 2026-05-14 Re: [PATCH 00/38] lockd: Convert NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen Neutral LLM (per-reviewer)

This patch addresses a regression introduced in the conversion of NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen, where the nlm4svc_lookup_file function was not properly zeroing the remainder of the file handle buffer after copying only the necessary bytes. This caused non-deterministic hash values for file handles shorter than 32 bytes, leading to lock state lookup failures. The patch adds a memset operation to ensure the entire buffer is zeroed before use.

2 reviewer comments — 0 participants
0 participants
Chuck Lever ↳ replying to Jeff Layton Reviewed-by Needs Work LLM
Reviewer noted that nlm4svc_lookup_file() introduced a regression by copying only fh.len bytes of the file handle without zeroing the remainder, leading to non-deterministic hash values and lock state lookup failures for handles shorter than 32 bytes. The reviewer suggested adding memset() to fix this issue.
Show original comment 
This appears to be a regression introduced by the (previous) NLMv4
series, and does not affect the current NLMv3 xdrgen conversion.

I can post a fix for this later today. Note that memset() was
removed as part of the xdrgen conversion because it zeroes much
more memory than actually needs to be initialized.


-- 
Chuck Lever
View on lore ↗
Signals: regression, fix
Jeff Layton (author) ↳ replying to Chuck Lever Needs Work LLM
Author acknowledged that the new code introduced a bug by removing protections that made it safe to skip zeroing, agreed to fix it in a later patch.
Show original comment 
I couldn't quite tell. It almost sounded like the change in the
original patch was safe, but this series removed some protections that
made it safe to skip the zeroing? In any case it sounds like a real bug
that we should probably fix, though it can be done in a later patch.

The series itself seems mostly mechanical. You can add:

Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
